checks.a_analysis: The output contains all headings, committee acronyms, goals, and table content found in the source PDF. No fabricated text or sections were identified. The metadata title is an expected AI addition for accessibility.
No phone numbers, email addresses, or URLs are present in the source document. No fabricated contact information was found in the output.

The output faithfully captures all sections from the three pages of the source PDF. Goal 1 (sections 1.1-1.3, 1.5-1.8) and Goal 2 (sections 2.1-2.4) are all present. Note that section 1.4 is absent in the source document itself, so its absence in the output is correct.

The source document uses multiple small tables for each numbered goal section. The output successfully consolidates these into larger tables per page while retaining every data row. Page 1 has 7 rows (headers and data), Page 2 has 8 rows, and Page 3 has 8 rows, matching the count of items in the source.

checks.check_name: Significant text omission within a section
checks.a_analysis: No significant text content was omitted. All committee descriptions, goal text, and implementation details within the tables are present in the output.

checks.check_name: Minor text differences beyond acceptable reformatting
checks.a_analysis: The text in the output matches the source content. Minor differences in punctuation (e.g., dashes vs hyphens) and whitespace are within acceptable reformatting standards. Meaning and information are preserved exactly.

checks.a_analysis: The content in the output correctly reflects the content of the source without extraneous duplication. While 'Goal 1' is repeated on Page 2, this matches the source document's layout where the header is repeated on the second page of the goal section.

The heading hierarchy is logical and does not skip levels. H1 is used for the title, and H2 is used for major sections like 'Committee Acronyms'. While there is inconsistency in how 'Goal' titles are tagged (Goal 1 on Page 1 and Goal 2 on Page 3 are paragraphs, while Goal 1 on Page 2 is an H2), there are no skips in the hierarchy (e.g., H1 to H3).

The table structure is corrupted across all three pages. The source document uses sub-goal headers (e.g., 1.1, 1.2, 2.1) as full-width category rows within the table. In the JSON output, these rows contain only a single cell with 'colspan: 1', whereas the subsequent data rows contain two cells. This results in a structurally inconsistent table where the column count varies between rows without appropriate spans, making the table layout invalid for programmatic consumption.
checks.b_evidence_source: Visual layout of tables on pages 1, 2, and 3 where blue-shaded header rows (e.g., 1.1, 1.2, 1.5, 2.1) span across two columns of data below them.
checks.b_evidence_output: Within the table elements (reading_order 6, 8, and 10), rows containing sub-goal text (e.g., '1.1. Promote...') have only one cell with 'colspan: 1', while subsequent data rows in the same table have two cells.

checks.a_analysis: The reading order follows the logical flow of the document from top to bottom, including correctly sequencing the repeated 'Goal 1' header on page 2.

The 'Committee Acronyms' list on Page 1 is converted to a single paragraph with newlines. While it loses specific list item tagging, the semantic meaning and visual structure are preserved in a readable format.
No external links were identified in the source document, and none were incorrectly injected into the output.

checks.a_analysis: The visual organization of the document is faithfully maintained. The HTML output accurately reflects the source PDF's structure, including the title, committee acronyms, and the categorized goals (Goal 1 and Goal 2) with their corresponding sub-points. The content flows in the same logical order as the original document. Although the HTML includes an added Table of Contents for navigation, this is an expected accessibility feature and does not constitute a layout failure.

The source PDF uses a structured layout for the goals (1.1, 1.2, etc.) that functions as a table with columns for descriptions and committee responsibilities. In the HTML output, these are rendered as valid tables. The columns are aligned, the text is contained within the appropriate cells, and the logical relationship between the initiative and the committee action remains clear. The visual distinction between the sub-heading (blue bar in PDF) and the data rows is maintained through layout and bolding in the HTML.
The source PDF is entirely text-based and contains no meaningful images, logos, or charts. Therefore, no images are missing from the HTML output.

There is no visual duplication of body content. While the document titles and goal headings appear in the Table of Contents at the top of the HTML page and again in the main body, this is an intentional accessibility feature for navigation (WCAG landmarks) and is not considered a defect. The actual content within the tables (e.g., specific committee tasks) only appears once.
